topic: Cross-reference integrity — do all section cross-references still say what they claim?
methodology: Systematic audit of all cross-references in the rendered docs/index.html (1,346 KB) and the underlying sections.json (13 parts, ~572 KB of section HTML). Steps: (1) Extracted all 310 id='...' definitions from the rendered HTML. (2) Extracted all 451 anchor <a href='#...'> cross-references across the site. (3) Confirmed zero hrefs point to undefined ids (all target anchors resolve). (4) Built a target-id → heading text map using the first h1/h2/h3 after each id definition. (5) For each anchor whose link text claimed a specific section number (e.g., 'Section 1.5', 'Part 6.6'), compared the claimed number against the target heading's leading number — flagged mismatches as semantic drift. (6) Cross-checked the V6 restructure map (2026-04-07) for old section numbers that should have been translated but weren't (searched for 'Part 4.5', 'Part 4.6', 'Section 4.5.1..9', 'Section 4.6.1..4', 'Section 3.5.1..9', 'Part 3.5', 'Part 3.3' etc.). (7) Separately counted links whose href goes to a part-level anchor (#partN) while the link text claims a specific subsection (X.Y) — these are imprecise but usually functional. (8) Verified the main heading structure in every part (part1..part10, part8b) by listing all h2 headings and confirming numbering continuity.

holes_found.description: part6 contains a broken cross-reference with both a wrong href and a stale sub-label: Section 1.8 (Refraction Cascade). The href #part1b navigates to 'Part 1b: Version Change Analysis' (a V50.6 → V51.0 changelog), not to the intended Section 1.8 which is 'The Coordinate Collapse: One Fix Destroys the Map' in Part 1 (the correct anchor is #section-1-8, which exists). Additionally, the parenthetical sub-label 'Refraction Cascade' is stale — current Section 1.8 is titled 'The Coordinate Collapse'. A reader clicking this lands on the wrong page entirely and sees unrelated content, which undermines the WIN-010 argument that 'WIN-010's underlying EM coupling mechanism was independently classified as Self-Contradicted in Section 1.8'. Full context: 'WIN-010's underlying EM coupling mechanism was independently classified as Self-Contradicted in Section 1.8 (Refraction Cascade)'.
holes_found.recommendation: In part6, change href from '#part1b' to '#section-1-8' and relabel '(Refraction Cascade)' to '(The Coordinate Collapse)' or '(Coordinate Collapse + refraction cascade)'. This is a functional bug, not a style issue — the link doesn't go where the text promises.

holes_found.description: 6 anchors in parts 5, 7, 8, 9 use the link text 'Section 1b' to point at the #section-1-5 anchor. The #section-1-5 anchor is the heading '1.5 Aetheric Refraction: The Model's Universal Correction Factor' (still numbered 1.5 in the current document). There is no heading titled 'Section 1b' or '1b' anywhere in the document — only a part-level 'Part 1b: Version Change Analysis' exists. Distribution: part5 (1x), part7 (3x), part8 (1x), part9 (1x). Meanwhile, part1 itself uses the correct text 'Section 1.5' to link to the same anchor (3x). This is an internal naming drift from the V6 restructure: the map's entry '"1.5": "1b"' was applied to 4 parts' link text but was never applied to the actual section heading or to part1's own references. A reader reading 'see Section 1b' will search for a heading named 1b, find Part 1b (Version Change Analysis), and be confused when the link lands on Section 1.5 Aetheric Refraction instead. A dome defender reading cover-to-cover can trivially point at this as evidence of sloppy editing.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) change all 6 'Section 1b' link-text instances in parts 5, 7, 8, 9 to 'Section 1.5' so they match the current heading (recommended — heading is canonical), or (b) renumber the heading to '1b Aetheric Refraction' inside part1 and update part1's 3 references, plus update the V6 map comment. Option (a) is lower-risk and consistent with all other Section 1.N references in the document.

holes_found.description: 21 anchors across 8 parts use the pattern href='#partN' with link text claiming a specific subsection (X.Y) — e.g., text='Section 2.9' href='#part2', text='Section 6.6' href='#part6', text='Section 4.1' href='#part4'. These links navigate the reader to the correct tab/part but drop them at the top of that part, not at the claimed subsection. Readers must then scroll or ctrl-F to find the subsection. The pattern is internally consistent (most instances target Section 2.9 → #part2, which is an extremely frequent cross-reference) and arguably intentional given the tab-switching JS (showTab('selftest')) — but several subsection anchors DO exist (#p6-66-5 for 6.6, #p6-62-1 for 6.2, #section-2-9 for 2.9, etc.) and would give a better reader experience if used. Worst offender: 'Section 6.6' and 'Section 6.2' are explicit subsection targets that both exist as named anchors.
holes_found.recommendation: Not required to fix, but quality improvement: where a specific subsection anchor exists (p6-NN-M, section-N-M), use it instead of the part-level fallback. Highest-value targets: (1) 'Section 6.6' → '#p6-66-5' in part1 (accuracy-claim cross-ref, high-traffic context), (2) 'Section 6.2' → '#p6-62-1' in part6's own self-ref, (3) 'Section 2.9' → '#section-2-9' if that anchor exists (verify — otherwise keep #part2). Leaving as-is is acceptable if the decider prefers not to churn 21 edits for UX polish.

advocate_mode.best_defense: A dome defender examining our review's cross-reference hygiene could argue: 'The #part1b→Section 1.8 mismatch looks like proof of a hasty rewrite — if you can't even keep your own anchor references straight, why should anyone trust your methodology when you accuse us of post-hoc retrofitting? And the Section 1b/Section 1.5 split is exactly the kind of editorial drift we're supposedly being criticized for: you moved things around, the numbering didn't follow, and now you've left 6 stale references that point to something they don't name.' They could extend this: 'The OP curmudgeon C1 pass declared 153/153 cross-references clean — either that audit was slapdash, or the document has degraded since. Either way, the quality claim in the review's own methodology section is refuted by its own file.'
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Fix the critical href bug (part6 #part1b) immediately and either normalize all 'Section 1b' text to 'Section 1.5' or add a clarifying footnote in Part 1 explaining the 1.5/1b dual naming. Then add a line to the methodology notes acknowledging that cross-reference audits are run periodically and that the V6 restructure introduced controlled drift that is being worked out over cycles. This turns 'you can't keep your numbering straight' into 'we audit our own document on a schedule and fix what we find, which is what we accuse the dome of not doing.'

related_wins: N/A — holistic review covers full document, not per-WIN

claims_checked: Total anchor count in rendered HTML: 451 (machine-counted)
Semantic mismatches (link text number ≠ target heading number): 7 unique instances across parts 1, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9
Imprecise part-level hrefs when subsection anchors exist: 21 unique instances

Instances of old V6 numbering (Part 4.5, Part 4.6, Section 4.5.X, Section 4.6.X, Section 3.5.X, Part 3.5, Part 3.3) still present in current content: 0 (the one 'Part 4.6' reference in part8b is correctly pointing at the NEW Part 4 Section 4.6 'Registry Framing Audit', not the old Part 4.6 Code Analysis)
quantitative_verification.computations: Counted via node scripts /tmp/full_xref_audit.js (anchor vs id), /tmp/sem_xref_v2.js (semantic mismatch), /tmp/subsec_href_audit.js (part-level fallback), /tmp/old_ref_scan.js (V6 stale numbers). All four scripts cross-checked against the fresh git clone at HEAD.

deeper_analysis: This cycle-2 review supersedes cycle-1 (2026-04-06, 153/153 clean) because the V6 restructure on 2026-04-07 renumbered sections and introduced the potential for drift, and many piecemeal edits have since landed (EXPs 129, 144, 162, 176, 181, 183, and others, plus the addition of Part 8b 'Fair-Auditor List' and kill-shot page rewrites). The anchor-level audit still passes perfectly — zero broken hrefs — which is a strong signal that the build pipeline and reviewers are catching dangling references. The failures are all SEMANTIC: link text making claims about the target that aren't borne out by the target heading. The #part1b/Section 1.8 bug is the only one that outright misdirects the reader (sending them to Version Change Analysis instead of Coordinate Collapse); the 'Section 1b' naming drift is confusing but functionally fine because the href still resolves to the right content. The 21 part-level fallbacks are a legitimate stylistic choice — many sections use #partN with JS tab-switching and rely on the reader's scroll or ctrl-F, which is defensible — but where named subsection anchors exist, they would improve UX without any architectural cost. No old V6 numbering (Part 4.5, 4.6, 3.5, etc.) was found in current prose, which is a notable win: the restructure translation has been applied thoroughly. Recommendation: fix the one critical href bug and the 6 Section 1b text instances; leave the 21 imprecise-href cases for the architecture-refactor wishlist.

citations_verified: All 310 id definitions in docs/index.html confirmed present
V6 restructure map section_numbers entries reconciled against current sections.json headings
citations_unchecked: Inline external URLs (DOIs, NASA links, etc.) out of scope for a cross-reference audit — see per-WIN citation checks

priority_queue_for_decider.fix: In part6, change Section 1.8 (Refraction Cascade) → Section 1.8 (The Coordinate Collapse)
In part5, part7 (3x), part8, part9 (1x each), change link text 'Section 1b' to 'Section 1.5' (href '#section-1-5' stays)
Consider replacing part-level #partN hrefs with specific #p6-66-5, #p6-62-1, #section-2-9 etc. where subsection anchors exist — 21 instances, UX polish only

followup_holistic: After decider patches the major/critical findings above, re-run this audit as cycle-3. The automated scripts (/tmp/*.js captured in this review's methodology) can be re-used.
